items.question: Explain Multinomial Naive Bayes for text classification in two sentences.
items.answer: Each document is a vector of word counts. The model estimates class-conditional word probabilities θ_{y,i} (with smoothing), then picks the class maximizing log P(y) plus the sum of count_i · log θ_{y,i}, assuming conditional independence of words given the class.

items.question: When would you choose Bernoulli NB over Multinomial NB?
items.answer: Choose Bernoulli NB for short texts where presence or absence of keywords matters more than repetition, using binary bag-of-words features. Multinomial NB is better when term frequency and document length carry signal via count features.

items.question: Why is Laplace smoothing necessary for text Naive Bayes?
items.answer: Training often yields zero counts for some word-class pairs. Without smoothing, θ_{y,i}=0 makes log θ_{y,i} undefined or −∞, zeroing the posterior when that word appears. Adding α to counts keeps all probabilities positive.

items.question: What sklearn components form a minimal Multinomial NB text pipeline?
items.answer: A Pipeline with CountVectorizer (optionally max_features and stop_words) followed by MultinomialNB(alpha=...). Fit on training strings and labels; transform test strings with the fitted vectorizer before predict.
